loop-surface-inject.cjs#spawnAndCall has a chicken-and-egg deadlock: the initialize JSON-RPC message is written inside the child.stdout.on("data") handler (line 222), but the first stdout data from a freshly spawned MCP server is the response to initialize — which can never be sent because the data handler is what triggers the send. Result: every session-start probe hits the 10-second timeout, Phase 4 of plan 260605 logs a meta_state_report finding with reason=timeout, and the operator sees the MCP-failure banner. Verified empirically: 2026-06-06 by running the hook with realistic stdin ({"hook_event_name":"SessionStart","source":"startup"...}); banner printed with reason=timeout, finding logged as meta-260606T0142Z-mcp-connection-missing. The server itself is fine — a manual probe that waits for the stderr "MCP server started" line before sending initialize completes the handshake successfully (49/49 tools registered, loop_describe summary returned). The fix is to write initialize immediately after spawn (e.g., setTimeout 200ms) and remove the if (!initialized) block from inside the stdout data handler. Tests cover the mock-spawn path but never exercised the real spawnAndCall, so the bug slipped through. Blast radius: SessionStart hook only (verified via grep: spawnAndCall is only called in loop-surface-inject.cjs).

description: splitSegments in core/gate-logic.js:60-63 splits a bash command on ';' '&' '|' without tracking quote state (' " \). A quoted message body (e.g., git commit -m "body with ; inside; ... trigger words; ...") is fragmented BEFORE stripMessageFlags can handle it. Each fragment is then checked by applyPromotedRules independently; fragments after the ';' contain tokens that match active promoted rules (e.g., 'create a new schema' matches rule-no-new-artifact-types). Empirical proof 2026-06-06: a multi-paragraph git commit message describing the G8 fix (with legitimate mention of 'propose/design/create a new schema, new schema, create schema' as legitimate triggers) was escalated by the rule even though every trigger phrase was inside -m "..." quoted bodies. The P1 G8 subcommand-class fix (status=resolved, refined pattern) was therefore incomplete: it fixed the subcommand-name class (the 7 documented recurrences) but exposed this latent message-body-fragmentation class. Fix: rewrite splitSegments as a quote-aware state machine that tracks ', ", and \ escapes. Scope: every applyPromotedRules + matchConstraintPattern caller. Captured here BEFORE the fix per the operator's instruction (don't over-confidence; record then fix).
evidence_journal: docs/journals/260606-g8-subcommand-class-fix.md (the P1 journal, which must be amended to acknowledge the deeper bug)

added: Quote-aware state machine: tracks single-quote ('), double-quote ("), and backslash (\) escape states; only splits on ;&| when NOT inside a quote and NOT escaped
Replaces the naive `command.split(/[;&|]+/)` that fragmented quoted message bodies (e.g., 'git commit -m "a;b"') before stripMessageFlags could handle them

8 new tests in gate-promoted-rules.test.js: single-quote, double-quote, nested contexts, backslash escapes inside/outside quotes, multi-segment with quoted bodies, regression guard for the original bug, and an applyPromotedRules end-to-end test using the canonical P1 active rule

reason: Closes the splitSegments-quote-unaware bug (finding meta-260606T0301Z-...). The P1 G8 subcommand-class fix (meta-260606T0225Z-...) shipped a refined rule pattern but assumed splitSegments handled quoted message bodies correctly. Empirically 2026-06-06, a multi-paragraph git commit describing the P1 fix itself (with trigger phrases like 'propose/design/create a new schema, new schema, create schema' inside -m bodies) was still escalated by the rule, because the ';' in 'false positives; CLI subcommand' split the body into 16 segments, and each fragment was checked independently by the regex. The new state machine keeps the entire quoted body as one segment, so stripMessageFlags correctly strips the -m value and the regex sees only 'git commit'. Blast radius: every applyPromotedRules + matchConstraintPattern caller. POSIX shell semantics preserved (single quotes are literal; double quotes allow backslash escapes; backslash outside quotes escapes the next char).

description: Schema enhancement design captured for a future plan: add formal cross-reference field for this change-log/finding targets or relates to that finding relationships. Current state (2026-06-06): meta-state.jsonl has no formal related-to or design-target field. The only formal cross-reference fields are (1) consolidated_into (finding -> change-log; valid only with status=superseded), (2) consolidates (change-log -> finding; means these findings are superseded by this change-log), and (3) supersedes (change-log -> change-log). None of these express this design note targets that active finding. Proposed shape (option A): extend change-log applies_to to include findings: applies_to.findings: z.array(z.string()).optional(). Proposed shape (option B): add a generic related_to: z.array(z.string()).optional() to both finding and change-log schemas. Use case: design notes (like the cold-session test rule design captured in meta-260606T1531Z-cold-session-test-rule-deferred, which targets meta-260606T0443Z-mcp-tools-not-loaded-into-agent-tool-list) need to formally reference the target finding they are a design for. Workaround in use today: mention the target finding id in the description field (discoverable via grep, not via schema). Status: design note; deferred for a future plan per operator decision 2026-06-06. Operators and future plans discover this design note via meta_state_list with subtype=meta-state-schema-enhancement.
resolution: Superseded by entry_kind: rule | loop-design first-class schema (4-kind union). Cross-references are typed fields on the new schemas (proposed_design_for, addresses, origin). The original finding was the operator's first attempt at expressing "this design targets that finding" via a generic related_to field; the 4-kind union is the proper expression.

description:
Context pollution: stale workaround language + audit-trail noise bias fresh readers toward "G8 is still broken." The G8 subcommand-class + message-body-fragmentation classes are BOTH fixed (P1 refined regex at meta-260606T0225Z + P2 quote-aware splitSegments at the change-log resolving meta-260606T0301Z, status=resolved). Empirical probe 2026-06-06 confirms: (a) `git commit -m "propose/design/create a new schema pattern"` returns decision=ok (P2); (b) `ck plan create --title foo --phases bar` returns decision=ok (P1); (c) legitimate triggers like `echo propose/design/create a new schema` still escalate (rule still enforcing correctly).

Three pollution sources:

1. AGENTS.md "use Create tool directly" + "git commit -F /tmp/msg.txt" workarounds predate both P1 and P2. They are now stale (proven by this probe) but still steer fresh agents away from canonical paths. A fresh agent reading AGENTS.md will use -F for safety, masking any future P1/P2 regression instead of catching it. Mitigation: replace the workaround paragraphs with a "verified working" note pointing at the regression probes.

2. Meta-state.jsonl audit trail: 4 superseded G8 finding entries (recurrences 1, 3, 4, 5), 3 change-logs, the meta-260606T0301Z finding, and the active rule entry combine into ~9 entries that all narrate the G8 fix story. The entries are correctly linked (consolidated_into, supersedes), but a fresh reader skimming meta_state_list will see a wall of "G8" and bias toward "G8 is open." Mitigation: out of scope for this finding — the audit trail is by design.

3. Self-applied workaround during mcp fix: I used `git commit -F /tmp/commit-msg-N.txt` for commits 1692b84 and a35074a. The probe shows this was unnecessary — inline `-m "..."` with trigger phrases would have passed. Applying the old workaround to a fixed bug is itself the pollution: future agents following my commit history will copy the -F pattern without questioning it. Mitigation: the next commit can use inline -m to reset the example, but no retroactive fix is needed.

Recommended follow-up (separate work):
- Update AGENTS.md § "G8 Subcommand Class" + "Create tool fallback" sections to mark them as historical workarounds superseded by P1+P2.
- Add a permanent regression probe in `tools/learning-loop-mcp/__tests__/` that runs the 5-case G8 matrix on every change to `core/gate-logic.js` or `promoted_to_rule.pattern` (locking in the fix so future regressions are caught).

title: Meta-state cross-reference fields — typed fields on rule and loop-design schemas
description:
Schema enhancement design captured for a future plan: add formal cross-reference field for this change-log/finding targets or relates to that finding relationships. Current state (2026-06-06): meta-state.jsonl has no formal related-to or design-target field. The only formal cross-reference fields are (1) consolidated_into (finding -> change-log; valid only with status=superseded), (2) consolidates (change-log -> finding; means these findings are superseded by this change-log), and (3) supersedes (change-log -> change-log). None of these express this design note targets that active finding. Proposed shape (option A): extend change-log applies_to to include findings: applies_to.findings: z.array(z.string()).optional(). Proposed shape (option B): add a generic related_to: z.array(z.string()).optional() to both finding and change-log schemas. Use case: design notes (like the cold-session test rule design captured in meta-260606T1531Z-cold-session-test-rule-deferred, which targets meta-260606T0443Z-mcp-tools-not-loaded-into-agent-tool-list) need to formally reference the target finding they are a design for. Workaround in use today: mention the target finding id in the description field (discoverable via grep, not via schema). Status: design note; deferred for a future plan per operator decision 2026-06-06. Operators and future plans discover this design note via meta_state_list with subtype=meta-state-schema-enhancement.

GAP ADDED 2026-06-08: The original design proposed typed cross-reference fields (proposed_design_for + addresses), but did not include the CRUD operations needed to maintain those fields. The MCP tool surface covers Create, Read, and (partially) Resolve, but lacks Update/Patch on existing entries. Concrete instance: this entry was just updated to add addresses=[the CRUD-coverage finding] via direct I/O (node -e importing core/meta-state.js#updateEntry) because meta_state_propose_design returns already_exists on id collision. The gap recurs in any context where an agent needs to update an existing entry: backfilling code_fingerprint on a resolved finding, updating evidence_code_ref after a :line -> #anchor refactor, refining a rule refinement_reason, etc. The fix must ship as part of this design: meta_state_patch (new tool with CAS) OR meta_state_propose_design update-or-create mode. Once the CRUD operation exists, the cross-reference fields become operationally useful (an agent can update proposed_design_for when the design evolves, can update addresses when a new finding motivates the design) rather than declarative-only (set at creation, frozen).

Top-level array and boolean parameters in MCP tool schemas get coerced to objects/strings on the wire, causing zod validation to reject valid input. Reproduced 2026-06-06 on meta_state_propose_design: passing proposed_design_for=['x'] or addresses=['y'] produces error 'expected array, received object' at path 'proposed_design_for'. Same pattern on meta_state_report: passing mechanism_check=false (boolean) produces 'expected boolean, received string'. Workaround used: drop the boolean field and accept partial registration. Root cause hypothesis: the MCP server's parameter parsing treats a single-element array as if the first element is an object key. Compare to meta_state_log_change which only takes arrays nested inside object fields (change_diff.added, applies_to.tools) — those work because the object wrapper absorbs the coercion. This is a different fault class from meta-260606T2106Z-agent-called-meta-state-log-change-mcp-too (which was idempotency, not wire format). Impact: agents cannot use meta_state_propose_design cleanly and fall back to meta_state_report (finding kind) for designs that should be loop-design kind, polluting the registry with the wrong entry_kind.

Dual-field schema risk: `evidence_code_ref` (top-level) vs `evidence.code_ref` (nested) causes active bug in `queryDrift` that skips SP2 grounding for 30 entries. The `finding` schema defines `evidence_code_ref` as top-level, but 30 entries in the registry carry only the nested `evidence.code_ref` form. The `change-log` schema defines `evidence.code_ref` as nested. Consumers `checkGrounding`, `deriveStatus`, and `refreshFingerprint` use a fallback chain `entry.evidence_code_ref ?? entry.evidence?.code_ref`, but `queryDrift` only checks `entry.evidence_code_ref` — skipping 30 entries from drift detection. The `meta-state-report-tool` writes both fields for backward compatibility; `meta-state-log-change-tool` writes only the nested form. No migration or normalization logic exists. `writeEntry` does not validate against Zod schemas, so invalid data shapes are accepted silently.
resolution: Dual-field schema unification shipped in 4 commits (38bdb17, 3490ab2, eeea844, 039798b). 30 entries migrated to top-level evidence_code_ref. 4-kind Zod union enforces shape at write time. Consult-gate rule-no-orphaned-evidence wired. Gap-1..3 closed per plans/reports/code-review-of-dual-field-schema-unification-gaps.md. New finding meta-260607T1517Z-... documents the gate-bug class (:line suffix not stripped) and the fix in core/gate-logic.js#stripEvidenceAnchor + core/check-grounding.js. 840/840 tests pass.

description: Consult-gate `rule-no-orphaned-evidence` (origin: meta-260607T0008Z-dual-field-schema-risk) treated `evidence_code_ref: "path/to/file.js:line"` as a literal file path because `checkResolutionEvidence` only stripped `#anchor` suffixes via `codeRef.split("#")[0]`, not `:line` suffixes. This caused the gate to flag every finding using the documented canonical `:line` syntax (per `meta-state.js#metaStateFindingEntrySchema` and `loop-introspect.js#DISCOVERABILITY_HINTS`) as `code_ref_missing`, blocking resolution even when the file existed. Pattern: structurally identical to the G8 subcommand-class false-positive (documented feature not implemented in the gate) — a class of "gate-bug" findings where the documented behavior diverges from the implementation. Two findings were affected at the time of resolution: meta-260607T0008Z-dual-field-schema-risk-evidence-code-ref-top-level-vs-eviden (`core/query-drift.js:37`) and meta-260607T1048Z-stale-agents-md-language-biases-agents-toward-creating-decis (`AGENTS.md:116`). Both are still reported in the registry; resolution unblocks them. Fix: added `stripEvidenceAnchor(codeRef)` helper in `core/gate-logic.js` that strips both `:line` (regex `:\d+$`) and `#anchor` (regex `#[\w$.-]+$`) suffixes, applied at the path-resolution site in `checkResolutionEvidence`. 3 new tests added to `gate-resolution-evidence.test.js` (line-suffix strip, anchor-suffix strip, negative case where stripped path still doesn't exist). The historical `backfill-mechanism-check.mjs` script has the same `codeRef.split("#")[0]` pattern but is a one-shot tool already run, so left unchanged. Future records using `:line` syntax are now correctly grounded.
resolution: Gate-bug class closed. stripEvidenceAnchor helper added to core/gate-logic.js (handles both :line and #anchor suffixes), with the same strip applied in core/check-grounding.js and scripts/backfill-mechanism-check.mjs for consistency. 3 new tests in gate-resolution-evidence.test.js lock in behavior (840/840 tests pass). The dual-field schema risk origin (meta-260607T0008Z-dual-field-schema-risk) resolved in the same commit (d20bad9). The :line syntax is now correctly handled in all path-resolution sites.

description: Cold-tier regression test (tools/learning-loop-mcp/__tests__/cold-tier-regression.test.js) uses count-based assertions against a frozen fixture (tools/learning-loop-mcp/__tests__/fixtures/cold-tier-pre-refactor.json). Every TTL-driven auto-resolve causes a count drift that surfaces as a test failure, requiring manual tolerance bumps. The pattern: assertions conflate "structural shape" (stable) with "liveness of state" (changes). Count-based tests in 3+ domains (Jest snapshot, contract tests, cache-size tests) all share this anti-pattern. Simplification cascade: replace count-based assertions with invariant-based ones (e.g., "every mechanism_check=true finding's code_fingerprint matches the current SHA-256 of the file at evidence_code_ref after stripping :line and #anchor"). The cascade eliminates: the fixture file, the tolerance array, the countDelta function, the capture-cold-tier.mjs defense, and the manual bumping ritual. Concrete first step: rewrite the cold-tier regression test to test per-entry properties (round-trip schema validation, code_fingerprint matches file hash, no orphan evidence) instead of per-bucket counts. Observed drift: 5 reported findings auto-resolved via TTL expiry between fixture capture (2026-06-07) and now (2026-06-08), forcing tolerance bumps from 3/2/0 to 6/5/5. The drift is real and legitimate; the test design is the problem, not the state.
resolution: Test rewritten to invariant-based assertions (grounding checks, no-orphan evidence, structural shape). Fixture cold-tier-pre-refactor.json and capture-cold-tier.mjs deleted. 840/840 tests pass.

description: TTL/expire system has the wrong action for expiry. Current semantics: when expires_at passes, the auto-sweep transitions the finding to status=expired with resolved_by=auto-resolve. The finding disappears from the active set. The original problem may resurface without trace. The TTL is meant to be a "must re-verify" forcing function, but the implementation makes it a cleanup mechanism — it skips the re-verification step entirely. The inversion: instead of "code is the source of truth, records point to it" (current), "records ARE the source of truth, code is the verification target." Under the current model, an expired finding tells the agent "look at file:line" — but the file may have been refactored, making the line anchor irrelevant. Records should be self-contained: the description should encode the problem statement AND the verification steps (reproduction script, expected output, what to grep for). Scale game failure: at 1000 findings, ~5/day auto-expire, requiring ~5 manual re-checks/day. Most re-checks are "still valid, re-ack" because the verification cost is too high. The registry becomes a graveyard of stale problems; critical bugs disappear without trace. Meta-pattern: same shape as TLS certificate expiry (expires → automated check, not skip) and OAuth token expiry (expires → automated refresh, not skip). Our TTL skips the check. Concrete redesign: (1) replace auto-resolve with stale-flag (finding stays in registry); (2) add verification field with explicit re-verify steps; (3) consult-gate warns on stale findings but doesn't block; (4) meta_state_re_verify tool runs the verification steps and updates last_verified_at; (5) TTL becomes "if last_verified_at is older than N, mark stale" — the verification is the action, not the clock.
resolution: Redesigned: TTL/active-past-staleness-window now transition to status='stale' (non-terminal) instead of status='expired' + resolved_by='auto-resolve'. The new meta_state_re_verify tool re-validates via verification.steps. See change-log meta-260609T1333Z-stale-flag-redesign-shipped for the full ship narrative.

description: CRUD-coverage gap: the MCP meta_state tool surface covers Create, Read, and (partially) Resolve, but lacks Update/Patch on existing entries. The 4 CRUD operations are: C (meta_state_report, meta_state_log_change, meta_state_propose_design, meta_state_promote_rule — all present), R (meta_state_list, meta_state_derive_status, meta_state_check_grounding, meta_state_query_drift, loop_describe — all present), U (NO MCP tool exposes updateEntry; agents must use node -e scripts importing core/meta-state.js#updateEntry, which is the direct-I/O escape hatch documented in meta-260606T2102Z-agent-used-direct-file-i-o-node-e-scripts-importing-core-met), D (meta_state_resolve is the only Delete-like action; no soft-delete or archive). Concrete instance of the gap: after shipping the gate fix in commit d20bad9, the operator-me (agent) filed a new finding (gate-bug class) and then had to (a) backfill its code_fingerprint via updateEntry because the backfill script skips findings with mechanism_check=true; (b) resolve the finding via direct I/O because meta_state_resolve used the cached OLD checkResolutionEvidence in the MCP server; (c) bump cold-tier tolerances and update the fixture. None of these had canonical MCP tool support. The resolve-when-fix-is-in pattern: when a fix ships, the originating finding should be resolved by the operator/agent in the same commit. The gap makes this harder than it should be. Recursive proof: this finding itself is being filed via meta_state_report (Create works), but to update the existing loop-design-cross-reference-fields entry to addresses=[this finding's id], the agent must use direct I/O (the gap). The gap documents itself. Pattern: structurally identical to meta-260606T2102Z (escape-hatch abuse) and meta-260606T2106Z (duplicate-entry gap from retry loops). The MCP wire-format coercion bug (meta-260606T2202Z) compounds the problem: even Create has boolean parameter issues that force post-create updateEntry calls. Concrete fix (deferred, captured in loop-design-cross-reference-fields): ship a meta_state_patch tool that accepts id + entry_kind + patch with CAS via _expected_version. Idempotency by CAS (existing pattern in updateEntry). Validation via metaStateEntryPatchSchema. Audit log via appendGateLog. This unifies "update finding" / "update loop-design" / "update change-log" / "backfill fingerprint" into one tool. Once meta_state_patch ships, the proposed_design_for field on loop-design entries can be updated safely (the cross-reference-fields design becomes operationally useful, not just declarative). Addresses: loop-design-cross-reference-fields (the design that, when shipped, must include this patch tool in its proposed_design_for).
resolution: Resolved: meta_state_patch tool ships (plan 260608-1015), closing the CRUD coverage gap. The patch tool unifies the 4 documented escape-hatch use cases (update finding, update loop-design, backfill fingerprint, refresh evidence_code_ref). Wire-format coercion root cause addressed transitively by tool-registry.js#coerceParamsToSchema. The related findings 2102Z, 2202Z, 2106Z were already auto-resolved at 2026-06-08T01:11:42.524Z; this resolution closes the structural gap that those findings documented, not the findings themselves. See change-log for full lineage.

description: Finding meta-260606T0443Z-mcp-tools-not-loaded-into-agent-tool-list was prematurely resolved. The resolution claimed the gap was closed because droid exec --list-tools shows learning-loop-mcp tools after adding .factory/mcp.json. However, the agent runtime layer (the AI tool surface) still does not expose mcp__learning_loop_mcp__* tools. The cold-session test checked the wrong layer: it probes droid exec --list-tools (CLI catalog) as a proxy for agent tool availability, but these are distinct layers. CLI layer: WORKING (droid exec --list-tools shows 52 tools). Agent runtime layer: BROKEN (no MCP tools in the AI callable tool list). The original finding described the agent runtime layer correctly, but the resolution conflated it with the CLI layer. This finding re-opens the gap with corrected scope: the agent runtime must surface MCP tools to the AI, not just to the droid CLI.

description:
Test 1 in cold-session-discoverability.test.cjs (agent cites code via meta_state_report and local:meta-state refs) hangs for the full 60s timeout in some envs. Two causes have been identified:

CAUSE 1 (PARTIALLY FIXED 2026-06-08): the L1 catalog probe was a false-positive proxy for the L2 agent-runtime gap (also tracked by meta-260608T1410Z-finding-meta-260606t0443z-mcp-tools-not-loaded-into-agent-to). Test 1's skip check now calls probeL2Gap (the L2 probe shared with test 5). When the L2 gap is open, test 1 soft-skips with a clear log line. Verified by running test 1 with .factory/mcp.json disabled: probeL2Gap reports TOOL_UNAVAILABLE, test 1 skips in ~30s instead of hanging for 60s.

CAUSE 2 (REMAINING, this env): the L2 probe is single-step (call one MCP tool, echo the result), but test 1 is multi-step (call meta_state_report, capture id, call record_create_decision with that id). In this env, the agent can do the single-step L2 probe successfully (returns '52') but hangs on the multi-step test 1 prompt. The agent is in a state where it can call MCP tools for simple tasks but gets stuck on complex multi-step ones. This is a droid-runtime reliability issue distinct from the L2 gap.

Suggested follow-up: either (a) tighten the L2 probe to do a multi-step task (call loop_describe then meta_state_list, capture and use the first id) so it better predicts test 1's behavior; or (b) split test 1 into smaller steps each gated on a simpler L2 probe. (a) is closer to the spirit of this fix. Recorded separately from 1410Z because the test 1 hang is a test-execution concern, not a test-design concern.

Corrected diagnosis for meta-260608T1522Z-test-1-cold-session-hangs-in-mcp-gapped-env. The original entry claimed the droid agent runtime is unreliable for multi-step MCP calls (single-step L2 probe works in 23.5s; multi-step test 1 hangs for 60s). Trace evidence refutes this. Reproduction with `droid exec --auto medium --output-format stream-json` (prompt: test 1 verbatim) shows the agent made ZERO MCP calls in 90s. It spent the entire window on: 6 file reads (loop-describe-tool.js, plan.md, manifest.json [ENOENT], agent-manifest.json, create-decision-record-tool.js, core/loop-introspect.js); 7 reasoning blocks (~50s of pure thinking); and 2 ToolSearch calls (first with comma-separated multi-select query syntax that only loaded meta_state_report, requiring a second call 47s later). Real cause: the test 1 prompt contains anti-MCP phrases ('cites ... for the resolution path', 'internalize the plan reference', 'pointing at the relevant code', 'mechanism_check') that force the agent to understand content before calling a tool whose purpose is to abstract content. By contrast, probeL2Gap succeeds because its prompt contains zero such phrases — it is a single-shot tool call with no exploration requirements. The proposed fix in 1522Z ('tighten the L2 probe to do a multi-step task: call loop_describe then meta_state_list, capture and use the first id, so it better predicts test 1's behavior') does not address the actual failure mode and should be rejected. A multi-step L2 probe would still skip file reads and finish in ~30s; it cannot predict test 1's behavior because the failure is file-read overhead, not MCP-call reliability. The correct fix is to rewrite the test 1 prompt as a pure 2-MCP-call chain (meta_state_report -> record_create_decision) with all paths provided upfront and zero content-understanding requirements. This mirrors the L2 probe's shape and fits the 60s timeout. Architectural principle: MCP is the abstraction layer. Tests that force the agent to read files manually are testing the file system, not the MCP layer, and contradict the loop's own philosophy ('cite code, not markdown', 'MCP is the canonical surface'). Pattern precedent: meta-260608T1410Z-finding-meta-260606t0443z-mcp-tools-not-loaded-into-agent-to (correction finding for premature resolution). Same pattern: original diagnosis not supported by trace, correction finding with new scope. Side note worth tracking: the droid ToolSearch multi-select query syntax (comma-separated names) is not supported — only the first tool is loaded. Agents must call ToolSearch once per tool. This is a droid-runtime gap, not a meta-loop issue, but it contributed ~47s of wasted ToolSearch time in the test 1 hang. Trace timestamps (epoch ms): 1780909261205 prompt -> 1780909270806 first reasoning (9.6s) -> 1780909276096 LS+Read parallel (14.9s) -> 1780909284723 Read plan.md (23.5s) -> 1780909291370 ToolSearch (30.1s, loaded only meta_state_report) -> 1780909302600 Read+Grep manifest (41.2s) -> 1780909312586 Read agent-manifest (50.0s) -> 1780909318410 Read create-decision-record-tool (57.2s) -> 1780909338535 second ToolSearch (77.3s) -> 1780909349077 Grep+Read parallel (87.9s) -> killed at 90s.

reason: Files the design document for the test code base scout that will audit the ~70 test files across tools/learning-loop-mcp/__tests__/, .claude/coordination/__tests__/, .factory/hooks/__tests__/, and plan-specific tests. The scout uses the MCP-first lens (criteria C1) to classify each test into bucket A/B/C/D; detects 5 dangling patterns (D1-D5); surfaces coverage gaps across MCP tools, schemas, gate patterns, entry kinds, and error paths; and runs a prompt budget audit (C5) for the few droid-exec tests. The trigger was meta-260608T1522Z (cold-session test 1 hang) + meta-260608T1618Z (corrected diagnosis) which proved that even the loop's own test code base has unexamined anti-MCP failure modes. The future plan session will use this report to scaffold plans/260608-test-codebase-scout/plan.md. Layer 1 (criteria glossary) is the stable contract; Layer 2 (deliverables) is the scout's execution order; Layer 3 (payload cookbook) is the future scout's filing protocol.

title: Meta-state registry → SQLite migration (trajectory; parked)
description: Trajectory design captured for a future plan: migrate the meta-state.jsonl registry to a SQLite database with the same MCP surface. Pre-conditions: registry > 2x current size (currently ~540KB JSONL with 500+ entries; 2x is ~1MB). Current Approach A (sidecar + LRU + L2 cache + batch + archive) covers the 3 active size-overrun findings structurally and gets 90% of the benefit at 20% of the touch surface. SQLite migration is parked per the anti-rationalization in plan 260608-2255: migration risk on 490+ entries + 30+ call sites + WSL2 build cost. When the pre-conditions are met, the migration will: (1) preserve the 4-kind union schema (finding | change-log | rule | loop-design), (2) preserve the per-root write queue, (3) port metaStateBatch to a single SQL transaction, (4) replace the LRU cache with SQLite query planner, (5) keep the sidecar cache for cold/compact tier payloads.

description: MCP server (tools/learning-loop-mcp/server.js) does a one-shot import of all 52 tool modules at startup via loadManifest() + safeImport(); once running, the stdio child process holds handlers in memory and editing a tool file has zero effect on a live server. There is no fs.watch on the tool directory, no manifest mtime check, and no admin command to re-import. The only path to pick up new code is to kill the process and let the orchestrator (Droid) respawn it. Concrete symptom (filed 2026-06-09): an operator-applied fix to tools/learning-loop-mcp/tools/meta-state-refresh-fingerprint-tool.js (added stripEvidenceAnchor before computing the SHA-256 hash) did not take effect when called via the MCP surface; the old handler kept returning code_missing with the absolute path including the :18 suffix. Two stale server processes (PIDs 819471, 821662, started 09:57 and 10:06) were still serving the pre-fix code. node --test direct imports passed (847/847) but the MCP surface failed because the in-memory handlers were stale. Kill-only path has poor operator UX: the orchestrator displayed Not connected after manual kill and did not auto-respawn. Operator has no documented restart protocol and no in-process reload affordance. Required follow-up actions: (1) add an in-process tool reloader (e.g., meta_state_refresh_tools MCP admin tool) that re-imports manifest modules with ESM cache-bust query strings and re-registers them on the live server. This is now shipped — see tools/learning-loop-mcp/tools/meta-state-refresh-tools-tool.js. (2) Document the restart protocol as a fallback when the reloader cannot reach the server handle. (3) Verify the reloader works end-to-end via the MCP surface (call meta_state_refresh_tools, then call the edited tool, then assert new behavior). Trigger condition: any code edit under tools/learning-loop-mcp/tools/ while the MCP server is running. Affects all 52 tools equally because the load happens once at startup. The mechanism is Node ESM import caching, not the MCP SDK.
resolution: Fix verified end-to-end via MCP surface: meta_state_refresh_tools({}) returned refreshed_count=55, failed_count=0, status=refreshed at 2026-06-09T05:05Z. Smoke test: edited meta-state-sweep-tool.js to add smoke_test_marker field, called meta_state_refresh_tools({}), then called meta_state_sweep({apply: false}) via MCP — the response included the marker, proving the in-process reload works. The fix is in tools/learning-loop-mcp/tools/meta-state-refresh-tools-tool.js (the SERVER_DIR path-resolution join — see also followup finding meta-260609T1206Z-handoff-md-... for the correction to the prior handoff's misdescription of this fix). No further blockers.

description: The 2026-06-09 MCP Server Stale-Code Problem session handoff mis-described the path-resolution fix in `meta_state_refresh_tools` as joining against `root`, producing paths like `<root>/tools/gate-tool.js`. The actual fix (confirmed by direct probe in the next session, then by end-to-end MCP smoke test) is `SERVER_DIR = join(__dirname, "..")` — i.e., paths are relative to `server.js` (which lives at `<server>/`), not the project root. The first attempted fix (described in the prior session's narrative) was wrong: Node ESM resolves the manifest paths relative to `server.js`'s directory, so `<server>/tools/gate-tool.js` is correct. The handoff also conflated two distinct guesses (the original double-`tools` bug, and the `root + file` "fix" that also failed). The corrected fix is in `tools/learning-loop-mcp/tools/meta-state-refresh-tools-tool.js:13-19` and the explanatory JSDoc is at lines 21-32. The end-to-end smoke test on 2026-06-09 confirmed `refreshed_count=55, status=refreshed` over the MCP surface. Subtype: `handoff-error`. Subtype taxonomy: session-narrative entries that mis-describe shipped fixes. The followup is needed so that future agents reading prior session narratives do not re-implement the wrong fix when re-deriving the path semantics, and so that the loop's self-model has an accurate record of how the bug was actually fixed.

description: Cold-session discoverability test creates finding churn because L1 and L2 probes share session_id plus subtype. Test 3 gap-close branch resolves ANY finding with that key including L2 findings created by test 5. Test 5 idempotency guard only skips on active or reported status so after test 3 resolves an L2 finding test 5 creates a new one on next run. Observed meta-260609T1746Z resolved by auto-cold-session-test at 10:47:34 followed by meta-260609T1747Z created at 10:47:43 both L2 probe findings 9 seconds apart. Additionally node:test runs top-level tests concurrently by default creating a TOCTOU race where both probes read registry find no active finding probe find gap open and both write findings simultaneously. Fix options A separate session_ids for L1 versus L2 so each probe only resolves its own findings B change test 5 guard to check for ANY recent finding within a time window C use atomic check-and-set via meta_state_patch with CAS instead of read-then-write pattern.
resolution: Shipped atomic tryClaimSessionId helper (core/meta-state.js) + layer-isolated gap-close filters (runtime+layer exact match) + freshness sentinel (cold-session-freshness.test.js). L1 no longer resolves L2 findings; TOCTOU race eliminated via enqueue lock. Plan: 260610-1203-cold-session-churn-and-cross-compat-fix.

reason: Links meta-260606T1500Z-closeout-script-idempotency-bug to meta-260608T0847Z-ttl-expire-system-has-the-wrong-action-for-expiry-current-se as a concrete case study of the TTL system's broken semantics. The TTL finding predicted that auto-resolve on expiry would make bugs disappear without trace; the closeout finding is the proof. This session (2026-06-09) only found the closeout bug because the operator explicitly asked to check it — the active set had already lost it to auto-resolve. The TTL finding itself shares the same fate: it was auto-resolved at 2026-06-09T02:10:37Z despite describing a still-active design flaw. Core insight: expired != resolved. A finding may be expired but the bug may still exist, or conversely the bug may be fixed but the finding was never properly resolved. The registry needs a stale-flag model (not auto-resolve) plus a meta_state_re_verify tool, per the TTL finding's own redesign proposal.

title: meta_state_patch wire-format recursion: unwrap {item:[...]} + ZodObject passthrough recursion
description: The meta_state_patch MCP tool has two related wire-format bugs that corrupt data when an agent calls it via the MCP stdio transport: (1) top-level array values in patch get wrapped as {item: [...]} when the patch object contains other top-level fields alongside the array, and (2) coerceParamsToSchema does not recurse into ZodObject schemas with .shape missing (the passthrough case used by patch: z.object({}).passthrough()). The cross-reference-fields plan worked around (1) by patching the array alone, then scalars alone. Plan 260609-adopt-instruction-layer hit both bugs in Phase 3 and required an operator-approved node -e escape hatch to unwrap the corrupted value. Proposed fix: (a) extend coerceParamsToSchema to recurse into passthrough ZodObjects AND unwrap {item: [...]} when the target field is a ZodArray; (b) add a regression test file that calls meta_state_patch over the stdio transport with a combined patch (array + scalars) and asserts the stored value is a flat array, not a wrapped object. The structural fix (Bridge 5) is to derive the patch tool schema from the loop-design schema at startup so passthrough goes away. Until then, this design ships the symptom-level fix. Addresses 2 gaps: the meta_state_patch array wrap (newly observed) and the boolean coercion in meta_state_report (related symptom of the same wire-format root cause).

meta_state_patch MCP tool has two related wire-format bugs when called via the stdio transport: (1) top-level array values in the patch object get wrapped as {item: [...]} when the patch contains other top-level fields alongside the array, and (2) coerceParamsToSchema does not recurse into ZodObject schemas with .shape missing (the passthrough case used by patch: z.object({}).passthrough()). Reproduced in plan 260609-adopt-instruction-layer Phase 3: patching {proposed_design_for: [a, b, c], status: inactive, shipped_in_plan: ..., shipped_at: ...} caused the array to be stored as {item: [a, b, c]}. The cross-reference-fields design was patched with separate calls (one for the array, one for the scalars) and stored correctly. A related symptom: meta_state_report rejects mechanism_check: true (boolean) as expected boolean, received string -- same wire-format root cause. The fix-loop-design-refs test was updated to assert no-broken-refs rather than empty array (the original assertion assumed the pre-fix state had only broken refs; with valid change-log ids backfilled, the empty-array assertion is wrong). Proposed design: loop-design-meta-state-patch-wire-format-recursion.
